data.title: CTO outlines raised 2026 core FFO of $2.06-$2.11 as structured investments target $175M-$250M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CTO Realty Growth, Inc. (CTO) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO John Albright said the quarter was “highlighted by a robust leasing and strong same-store NOI growth as well as the $81.6 million acquisition of a high-quality shopping center in Texas,” and reiterated the focus on “shopping centers located along growth corridors primarily in the Southeast and Southwest markets.”
  • President, CEO & Director John Albright reported leasing, saying, “we executed leases, renewals and extensions totaling 153,000 square feet, including 146,000 square feet of comparable leases at an average cash rent increase of 14%,” and added that this brought the portfolio to “95.4% leased.”
  • President, CEO & Director Albright highlighted embedded growth, stating, “our Signed-Not-Open pipeline totaled $6.2 million of annual cash base rent,” which he said “will provide a meaningful earnings tailwind beginning as we move through 2026 and into 2027.”
  • President, CEO & Director Albright detailed capital allocation actions: “we announced an acquisition of Palms Crossing… in McAllen, Texas for $81.6 million,” and also said, “Madison Yards located in Atlanta is under contract with a nonrefundable deposit, and we expect the sale to close in May.”
  • President, CEO & Director Albright said structured investments expanded after quarter end: “we completed a $75 million preferred equity investment… This preferred investment yields 12% and has a term of 2 years,” and he summarized the overall impact as “a weighted average yield of 11.6%.”
  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Philip Mays said, “For the first quarter, core FFO was $16.9 million… and on a diluted share basis was $0.52 per share,” and added, “AFFO was $18.2 million… and on a diluted share basis was $0.56 per share.”

Outlook

  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays raised full-year targets, saying, “we are increasing our core FFO outlook to a new range of $2.06 to $2.11 per diluted share and our AFFO outlook to a new range of $2.19 to $2.24 per diluted share.”
  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays listed key inputs as “structured investments of $175 million to $250 million,” “same-property NOI growth for shopping centers of 3.5% to 4.5%,” and “general and administrative expenses of $19.7 million to $20.2 million.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s initial 2026 outlook, management raised the per-share ranges (previously “$1.98 to $2.03 for core FFO” and “$2.11 to $2.16 for AFFO,” as stated by Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays on the Q4 call).

Financial Results

  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays attributed growth in profitability measures, stating, “The growth in both core FFO and AFFO was primarily driven by leases executed over the past year that have since commenced paying rent,” while noting “approximately $0.01 related to nonrecurring recovery benefits.”
  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays said property performance was led by retail: “same-property NOI for shopping centers increased 6.8%,” and “excluding the nonrecurring recovery benefits… still increased… 4.2%.”
  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays described noncore drag, saying total same-property NOI growth was “impacted by one tenant… vacating 98,000 square feet at our Albuquerque property,” and reiterated mitigation timing: the space “has been fully leased to the state of New Mexico, which is expected to commence paying rent in late 2026.”
  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays summarized leverage and funding actions: “total debt… $651.8 million,” “liquidity… $125 million,” and “leverage at 6.4x net debt to pro forma adjusted EBITDA,” and added, “we… issue[d] approximately 733,900 common shares… for total net proceeds of $14.2 million.”

Q&A

  • Jay Kornreich, Cantor Fitzgerald asked about the $75 million preferred investment rationale and funding; President, CEO & Director Albright replied, “We’ve already did the investment… it was one closing,” adding, “the Watters Creek was recycled into that,” and “we just use the balance sheet for the balance of it.”
  • Kornreich asked for an update on the remaining vacant anchor spaces; President, CEO & Director Albright said, “terms have been agreed upon moving to leases,” but “these things… go really slow,” adding, “conservatively… 3 months,” and “call it, 9 months or so to kind of get the tenant in place.”
  • Matthew Erdner, JonesTrading asked what would drive investment volume toward the high end; President, CEO & Director Albright said, “We do have structured investments… could happen here in the next 30 days,” and “we hope to be more active probably in the next kind of 4 months,” while Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays added, “if we complete some of the larger property acquisitions… it would push us up towards the higher end of the range.”
  • Erdner asked whether an outparcel pad (Forsyth) was in guidance; Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays said, “they won’t contribute to earnings in this year,” and it is “part of… $30 million of capital earning low double-digit yield unlevered.”
  • Craig Kucera, Lucid asked about a cap/target for structured investments as a share of the balance sheet; President, CEO & Director Albright said, “the cap… will definitely be below 20% and maybe more in line with the 15%.”
  • Kucera asked if yield ranges changed given a 12% preferred deal; President, CEO & Director Albright said acquisitions were “in the 7.5% to 8% range,” and structured finance “in the kind of 10% to 13% range.”
  • Kucera asked about leasing spreads given expirations; Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays said, “the spreads… continue in the range they’ve been,” and “for the full year, should be pretty good.”
  • Kucera asked about Signed-Not-Open timing/modeling; Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays said remaining pipeline is “a little more Q3, Q4 weighted,” and “you should get pretty much the full impact… in ’27,” with “one tenant… to early ’28.”
  • John Massocca, B. Riley asked if Madison Yards cap rate was ~6%; President, CEO & Director Albright responded, “It’s a little higher than that because of the AMC Theatres.”
  • Massocca asked if macro uncertainty is affecting leasing; President, CEO & Director Albright said, “There’s been no hesitancy… We have not seen any pullback whatsoever.”
  • Massocca asked about watch list changes; President, CEO & Director Albright said, “there’s been no notable change one way or the other on the watch list.”
  • Massocca asked how industry M&A affects CTO; President, CEO & Director Albright said, “helpful on our recycling side for sure, but not helpful on our acquisition side,” adding, “just being a little bit nimble is an advantage for us.”
  • Gaurav Mehta, Alliance Global Partners asked about Palms Crossing value-add and rents vs. market; President, CEO & Director Albright said, “the market rents are below market,” but “there’s not really any sort of play… anytime soon,” and pointed to “a little bit of vacancy” and “an outparcel that we didn’t pay any money for.”
  • Mehta asked whether Madison Yards is included in guidance assumptions; Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays answered, “No, we didn’t put a disposition volume out there.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral and detail-driven, focusing on capital allocation and modeling mechanics (e.g., exposure limits, yield ranges, pipeline timing).
  • Management’s prepared tone was confident, with President, CEO & Director Albright saying, “2026 is off to a great start,” and linking the raise to “increased guidance… to new ranges that imply approximately 12% growth at the midpoint.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, guidance language shifted from “initial earnings guidance” (Q4) to “increasing” outlook (Q1), while Q&A pressure remained centered on leasing timing, investment pacing, and capital recycling.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance increased quarter over quarter as Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays moved from Q4’s “$1.98 to $2.03” core FFO per share to Q1’s “$2.06 to $2.11,” and from “$2.11 to $2.16” AFFO per share to “$2.19 to $2.24.”
  • Structured investment expectations expanded from Q4’s “$100 million to $200 million” to Q1’s “$175 million to $250 million,” while the shopping-center same-property NOI growth assumption stayed at “3.5% to 4.5%.”
  • Capital recycling focus became more immediate with President, CEO & Director Albright stating Madison Yards “is under contract… and we expect the sale to close in May,” versus Q4’s broader discussion of potential asset sales to fund acquisitions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays flagged quarterly NOI sensitivity, saying, “$200,000 impacts quarterly growth by approximately 100 basis points,” and warned that “unusual and nonrecurring items… can occasionally skew our same-property NOI.”
  • Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays highlighted a remaining earnings drag from the Albuquerque vacancy, while stating it is “fully leased to the state of New Mexico” with rent expected “in late 2026.”
  • President, CEO & Director Albright emphasized execution timing risk in large-box leasing, stating national tenant processes “go really slow,” even where “terms have been agreed upon.”

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a start-of-year acceleration driven by leasing, a new $81.6 million Texas shopping-center acquisition, and a post-quarter-end $75 million preferred equity investment yielding 12%. The company raised full-year 2026 core FFO and AFFO guidance ranges, anchored by assumptions that include $175 million to $250 million of structured investments and 3.5% to 4.5% same-property NOI growth for shopping centers, while pointing to the $6.2 million Signed-Not-Open pipeline and outparcel developments as multi-year earnings contributors.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Yum! expects Taco Bell U.S. margins of 24.5%-25.5% while keeping Pizza Hut strategic review on track for 2026 completion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Yum! Brands (YUM) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "In support of that mission, we recently unveiled our Raise the Bar priorities to drive our next chapter of global growth." (CEO & Director Christopher Turner) "These priorities include battling for the future consumer, accelerating restaurant unit economics and reaching the full potential of Byte." (CEO & Director Turner)
  • "In Q1, KFC delivered 6% system sales growth." (CEO & Director Turner) "the brand is driving system sales through industry-leading development with 7% unit growth in Q1 and a record number of Q1 gross builds." (CEO & Director Turner)
  • "Taco Bell U.S. reported 8% same-store sales growth, meaningfully outperforming the industry." (CEO & Director Turner) "Same-store sales growth in the quarter reflects 3 percentage points of transaction growth" (CEO & Director Turner) and "the successful launch of the Luxe Value Menu." (CEO & Director Turner)
  • "digital mix increased to 63% and digital sales approached $11 billion." (CEO & Director Turner) "This quarter, Taco Bell U.K. became the first international market to roll out both the digital ordering and Smart Ops bundles." (CEO & Director Turner)
  • "In the first quarter, Yum! system sales grew 6%." (Chief Financial Officer Ranjith Roy) "We opened 1,030 new stores in the quarter." (CFO Roy)

Outlook

  • Management did not state EPS or revenue guidance on the call, so no comparison to analysts’ EPS or revenue estimates was included.
  • "We now expect Taco Bell U.S. restaurant level margins to be between 24.5% and 25.5%, reflecting better top line momentum and higher margins than originally planned from the acquired Taco Bell stores." (CFO Roy)
  • "For this year, we expect our full year interest expense to fall in the range of $510 million to $520 million, excluding any potential debt issuances." (CFO Roy) "full year ex special G&A growth, excluding Pizza Hut, is expected to be up mid-single digits." (CFO Roy)
  • "we expect Q2 to reflect high single-digit growth year-over-year ex special ex Pizza Hut G&A due to the timing of project spend." (CFO Roy) "we expect approximately $5 million of noncash closure expenses for Habit" (CFO Roy) "by making a small number of closures in subscale markets." (CFO Roy)
  • "we expect Q2 core operating profit of approximately $17 million." (CFO Roy) "we remain on track to complete the review in 2026." (CFO Roy)

Financial Results

  • Management did not disclose quarterly EPS or reported revenue figures during the call, so no comparison to analysts’ Q1 EPS or revenue estimates was included.
  • "In the first quarter, Yum! system sales grew 6%. We grew new units by 5% and same-store sales globally at 3%." (CFO Roy) "Digital sales approached $11 billion with digital mix reaching a new high now at 63%." (CFO Roy)
  • "Taco Bell U.S. achieved restaurant-level margins of 23.9%." (CFO Roy) "KFC restaurant level margins were 10.3%, up to 100 basis points year-over-year" (CFO Roy) and "driven by a 240 basis point increase in U.K. restaurant margins." (CFO Roy)
  • "Ex special G&A was $284 million and was up 4%." (CFO Roy) "Franchise and license expense was $43 million, $9 million higher year-over-year" (CFO Roy) "primarily due to the investment in marketing and innovation testing as part of the Hut Forward program." (CFO Roy)
  • "For the quarter, gross capital expenditures totaled $75 million." (CFO Roy) "Our net leverage ratio ended the quarter at approximately 3.8x." (CFO Roy) "we repurchased approximately 1.2 million shares for a total of $185 million." (CFO Roy)

Q&A

  • David Palmer, Evercore ISI: "any color as you look around the globe in terms of demand trends"; CEO Turner: "We're pleased with the progression in KFC International" and "over the last 4 quarters, we've seen acceleration on a 2-year same-store sales basis" while adding that "those countries that I mentioned are proof points that his strategy is working."
  • David Tarantino, Baird: "are you seeing any signs of pause related to the unit growth"; CEO Turner: "As we look at our unit development outlook, we have high confidence." CFO Roy: "the entire Middle East represents less than 150 units in our current pipeline" and "as of today, our partners in that region expect no change to plans."
  • John Ivankoe, JPMorgan: "how you're thinking about this potentially big overall efficiency and effectiveness opportunity"; CEO Turner: "first and foremost, we want to use AI to drive growth" and described "10 different AI agents" in KFC U.K., including one "helping with permitting" and "Judy" helping "build training programs."
  • Dennis Geiger, UBS: asked about Taco Bell momentum and Live Más Café; CEO Turner: "They raised their aspirations" and "set a very specific target to strive for $3 million AUVs by 2030" while adding, "we're already getting tremendous returns from the Live Más Café investments."
  • Gregory Francfort, Guggenheim: asked about KFC comp disclosure and Saucy; CFO Roy: "we've moved KFC U.S. from being a subsegment" and noted it is "materially less than 5% of Yum! operating profit ex Pizza Hut"; CEO Turner: "we are already earning a big return on the Saucy investment across the global business."
  • Andrew Charles, TD Cowen: asked about KFC development risk and Taco Bell cadence; CEO Turner: "overall, no change to KFC's unit development outlook" and highlighted Taco Bell loyalty, saying "We had 30% growth in loyalty sales year-over-year" while CFO Roy added "we've taken up guidance on margins."
  • Hyun Jin Cho, Goldman Sachs: asked about Chief Scale Officer; CEO Turner: "We do still intend to add that Chief Scale Officer role" and said "We're taking our time to make sure we get that right."
  • Andrew Barish, Jefferies: asked about buying Taco Bell stores; CFO Roy: "we made, as you know, opportunistic acquisitions that are relatively small" and said the Q4 deal was "128 restaurants out of a worldwide base of 60,000 restaurants" while adding, "we're going to do it selectively and strategically."
  • Jeffrey Bernstein, Barclays: asked about QSR positioning; CEO Turner: "when you deliver what consumers want and need, you can win with consumers in any environment."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative to neutral analyst tone, with recurring pressure-testing on geopolitical disruption and development pacing ("geopolitical issues"; "LPG shortages") and structural questions on post-Pizza Hut cost structure ("bending the curve on G&A").
  • Slightly positive management tone, anchored in repeated confidence language ("we have high confidence"; "no change"; "we remain confident") and specific operating levers (AI rollout, Byte expansion, unit economics initiatives).
  • Versus last quarter, management maintained a similar confidence posture on Raise the Bar and Pizza Hut review timing, while Q1 included more explicit capital return and AI productivity examples ("half the size" development team; "10 different AI agents").

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q4, management framed 2026 as "confident the rest of our portfolio will meet or exceed every component of our long-term growth algorithm" (CFO Roy, Q4 2025); in Q1, CFO Roy repeated that posture with "we remain very confident in our ability to meet or exceed each component of our long-term growth algorithm when excluding Pizza Hut" while raising a key profitability outlook item: "We now expect Taco Bell U.S. restaurant level margins to be between 24.5% and 25.5%." (CFO Roy, Q1 2026)
  • Pizza Hut timing stayed consistent, shifting from "we intend to complete the review of options this year" (CEO & Director Turner, Q4 2025) to "we remain on track to complete the review in 2026." (CFO Roy, Q1 2026)
  • Q&A emphasis moved from Byte adoption mechanics and post-review portfolio framing in Q4 to Q1 emphasis on unit development resilience amid conflict, Taco Bell durability in a "difficult consumer environment," and organizational productivity from AI.

Risks and Concerns

  • "While the Middle East conflict creates uncertainty globally" (CFO Roy) and "has included short-term delays to obtain government permits and procure equipment in select markets such as the UAE and Turkey." (CFO Roy) "At this time, we see no change to our KFC development plans for the year." (CFO Roy)
  • Habit profitability pressure remained tied to input costs: "the team continues working to mitigate the ongoing bottom line impact of inflation, particularly from beef prices." (CEO & Director Turner)
  • Pizza Hut remains in transition: "Both Q1 and Q2 core operating profit includes expense related to the onetime Hut Forward investment that we previously disclosed." (CFO Roy)

Final Takeaway

Yum! management emphasized a Q1 start anchored in 6% system sales growth, 63% digital mix with digital sales approaching $11 billion, and 1,030 gross openings, while highlighting KFC’s 7% unit growth and Taco Bell U.S. 8% same-store sales growth. For 2026, the most concrete update was an increased Taco Bell U.S. restaurant-level margin outlook to 24.5%-25.5%, alongside reiterated expectations for mid-single-digit ex special G&A growth (excluding Pizza Hut), $510-$520 million in interest expense (excluding potential debt issuances), and Pizza Hut Q2 core operating profit of approximately $17 million as the strategic options review remains targeted for completion in 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wingstop anticipates 2026 global unit growth of 15% to 16% while guiding for low single-digit same-store sales decline
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Wingstop (WING) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We believe 2026 is going to be a transformational year for Wingstop and remain extremely confident in the long-term opportunity in front of us,” said President, CEO & Director Michael Skipworth, while noting “our same-store sales result in Q1 was disappointing and fell below our expectations.”
  • “Atypical winter weather resulting in temporary restaurant closures in over 700 restaurants” and “elevated gas prices as a result of the conflict in the Middle East” were cited by President, CEO & Director Michael Skipworth as the main Q1 pressures, adding that “our same-store sales trend worsened during the quarter and resulted in a decline of 8.7%.”
  • On execution priorities, President, CEO & Director Michael Skipworth said the company is pursuing “strengthening our operations through the Wingstop Smart Kitchen, expanding our reach to new guests and launching our new and highly differentiated loyalty program,” and reiterated a long-term “AUV target of $3 million.”
  • Chief Financial Officer Alex Kaleida said, “System-wide sales increased 5.9% to $1.4 billion in the quarter,” and added, “total revenue increased 7.4% to $183.7 million versus the prior year.”
  • On capital returns, Chief Financial Officer Kaleida said, “our Board of Directors authorized and declared a quarterly dividend of $0.30 per share,” and also noted, “the Board of Directors also authorized an additional $300 million available for share repurchases.”

Outlook

  • “We have updated our full year outlook… and now anticipate same-store sales to be down low single digits,” said President, CEO & Director Michael Skipworth, while adding, “we believe our business can return to growth in the second half of the year.”
  • Chief Financial Officer Alex Kaleida provided cadence assumptions: “We’re anticipating somewhere in the mid-single-digit decline range for comps in the second quarter, followed by a gradual improvement into that low to mid-single-digit positive range for the second half.”
  • Chief Financial Officer Kaleida updated expense guidance: “We are also updating our full year SG&A outlook to a range of $146 million to $149 million, which includes $3 million of restructuring charges… and $28 million of stock-based compensation expense.”
  • The company reiterated development and other items: Chief Financial Officer Kaleida said it is “reiterating… global unit growth of 15% to 16%,” plus “net interest expense of approximately $43 million” and “depreciation and amortization of approximately $30 million.”

Financial Results

  • Chief Financial Officer Alex Kaleida said “Adjusted EBITDA… was $65.4 million,” and added, “Q1 net income was $30 million or $1.08 per diluted share,” with year-over-year net income impacted because “a nonrecurring gain of $92.5 million [was] recognized in the prior year associated with the sale of our U.K. brand partner, Lemon Pepper Holdings.”
  • On franchise-driven revenue, Chief Financial Officer Kaleida said “Royalty revenue, franchise fees and other increased $8.7 million to $87.5 million,” and company-owned restaurant sales “increased by $2.9 million to $33 million.”
  • Cost and margin commentary centered on company-owned operations: Chief Financial Officer Kaleida said “company-owned restaurant cost of sales decreased 110 basis points… to 74.9%,” “primarily driven by a 160 basis point decline in food, beverage and packaging costs.”
  • Chief Financial Officer Kaleida tied higher SG&A to one-time items: “SG&A increased $3 million… to $34.4 million, primarily driven by a $2.4 million nonrecurring restructuring charge related to the corporate realignment announced in January this year.”

Q&A

  • David Tarantino, Baird: “where you’re seeing some of the traffic loss” and “any tactical response… to stop the bleeding”; President, CEO & Director Skipworth: “we do think that that’s attributed to… reaction to gas prices,” and on value messaging, “highlighting flavor under $10… not necessarily discounting or anything like that.”
  • Jeffrey Bernstein, Barclays: asked about comp improvement and confidence in “return to positive in the second half”; President, CEO & Director Skipworth: “we did see an improvement in the trend to start Q2”; Chief Financial Officer Kaleida: “mid-single-digit decline… in the second quarter… low to mid-single-digit positive… for the second half.”
  • Jeffrey Bernstein, Barclays: asked whether to “temper unit growth”; President, CEO & Director Skipworth: “we actually saw our brand partner margins and profitability improve in the first quarter,” and “we feel like we’re growing at the right pace.”
  • Christopher O’Cull, Stifel: asked about brand partners developing “ahead of their contractual mandates”; President, CEO & Director Skipworth: “we discourage brand partners from developing ahead of that contractual commitment.”
  • Sara Senatore, BofA: asked about low-income mix and “price point value below… $10”; Chief Financial Officer Kaleida: “about 25% within our database,” and “they’re actually trading up into larger bundles.”
  • Danilo Gargiulo, Bernstein: asked about adherence to 10-minute standard and capital allocation; President, CEO & Director Skipworth: “the change is pretty drastic”; Chief Financial Officer Kaleida: “we do see ourselves in a place that’s closer to that 4x leverage range.”
  • Jon Tower, Citi: asked if franchisees are reluctant to “move down on price points”; Chief Financial Officer Kaleida: “we don’t believe… training a guest to come to you for a $3 menu item… is… who Wingstop is.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative, with repeated probing on comp deterioration, traffic losses, value posture, and whether unit growth should be moderated (e.g., “stop the bleeding,” and “tempering unit growth”).
  • Management: slightly positive in strategy framing but defensive on near-term demand, emphasizing external factors (e.g., “outside of our control”) while reiterating conviction in initiatives (e.g., “we have a high degree of confidence in this outlook”).
  • Versus last quarter: the tone shifted toward explaining a larger near-term comp decline (from Q4’s discussion of “stabilized” trends to Q1’s “disappointing” result), while keeping the same second-half recovery narrative tied to Smart Kitchen and Club Wingstop.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance moved down: Q4 2025 guidance was “flat to low single-digit percent growth” for domestic same-store sales; Q1 2026 guidance became “down low single digits,” explicitly reflecting “elevated fuel prices” and Q1 impacts.
  • Smart Kitchen discussion progressed from deployment/execution setup in Q4 to quantifying peak-period improvement in Q1 (e.g., “a 16-point improvement” in Friday/Saturday speed attainment and delivery satisfaction gains).
  • Q&A focus remained consistent on speed standards, delivery performance, loyalty impact, and value framing, but the current quarter added more direct scrutiny on traffic losses and macro sensitivity to fuel prices.

Risks and Concerns

  • President, CEO & Director Michael Skipworth cited demand pressure from “elevated gas prices” affecting “the lower-income consumer that our business overindexes to,” and weather disruptions that “resulted in temporary restaurant closures in over 700 restaurants.”
  • Execution risk was framed as change management: President, CEO & Director Skipworth said Smart Kitchen is “a meaningful operational transformation,” and “a better team member experience… but it is still a big change.”
  • Management’s mitigation emphasis was on operational and digital execution, including “targeting a launch of our order-ready tracker by the end of Q2” and preparing “for a national launch [of Club Wingstop] by the end of Q2.”

Final Takeaway

Wingstop management positioned Q1’s 8.7% same-store sales decline as largely driven by weather closures and a fuel-price shock to its lower-income skew, while keeping strategy centered on Smart Kitchen execution, a national Club Wingstop rollout by end of Q2, and continued mid-teens unit growth. The company updated full-year domestic same-store sales guidance to a low single-digit decline, maintained 15% to 16% global unit growth guidance, and emphasized capital returns through a $0.30 dividend and expanded repurchase authorization.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: EXL forecasts 2026 revenue of $2.3B-$2.33B while raising adjusted EPS to $2.18-$2.23
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ExlService Holdings, Inc. (EXLS) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "In the first quarter, EXL generated revenue of $570 million, up 14% year-over-year and adjusted earnings of $0.58 per share, an increase of 20% year-over-year." (Co-Founder, Chairman & CEO Rohit Kapoor)
  • "Data and AI-led revenues grew 28% year-over-year in Q1 and now represents 60% of the company total." (CEO Kapoor)
  • "In Q1, total operations grew 10% year-over-year... The reported digital operations revenue after that migration was down 2% year-on-year. This is by design." (CEO Kapoor)
  • "We saw strong performance across each of our 4 operating segments to start the year." (CEO Kapoor) "Insurance grew 13% year-over-year" and "Healthcare and Life Sciences grew 21% year-over-year" and "Banking, capital markets and diversified industries grew 8% year-over-year" and "International growth markets grew 13% year-over-year." (CEO Kapoor)
  • "For full year 2026, we are increasing our revenue guidance to a range of $2.3 billion to $2.33 billion" and "increasing our adjusted diluted EPS to $2.18 to $2.23." (CEO Kapoor)
  • "We delivered a strong first quarter with revenue of $570.4 million" and "Adjusted EPS was $0.58." (Executive VP & CFO Maurizio Nicolelli)

Outlook

  • "We now anticipate 2026 revenue to be in the range of $2.3 billion to $2.33 billion... This also represents an increase of $20 million at the midpoint, which includes a $2 million foreign exchange headwind from our previous guidance." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • "We anticipate our adjusted EPS to be in the range of $2.18 to $2.23... up from our previous guidance of $2.14 to $2.19." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • "Our guidance is going to take... into the account what's happening in the current macro environment and also the geopolitical uncertainties that are out there." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • "We don't really provide a breakup... between data and AI-led and digital operations." (CEO Kapoor) "But it would be fair to say that our digital operations will grow slightly below the company average, and our data and AI-led piece will be powering the growth of the overall company." (CEO Kapoor)

Financial results

  • "We delivered a strong first quarter with revenue of $570.4 million, up 13.8% year-over-year on a reported basis and 13.4% on a constant currency basis." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • "SG&A expenses as a percentage of revenue increased 20 basis points year-over-year to 20.4%, driven by investments in data and AI-led solutions." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • "Our adjusted operating margin for the quarter was 20.5%, up 40 basis points year-over-year, driven primarily by improved gross margins." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • "Our cash, including short- and long-term investments as of March 31 was $266 million, and our revolver debt was $417 million for a net debt position of $151 million." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • "We spent $13 million on capital expenditures and repurchased 4.4 million shares at an average price of $31 per share, totaling $136 million." (CFO Nicolelli)

Q&A

  • Bryan Bergin, TD Cowen: "Can you just dig in on the key assumptions for data and AI-led versus digital ops growth"; CEO Kapoor: "Some of the industries where we see a little bit of softness is on retail and on communication" and "it would be fair to say that our digital operations will grow slightly below the company average, and our data and AI-led piece will be powering the growth." (CEO Kapoor)
  • Bryan Bergin, TD Cowen: "Can you just comment any change in the expectation on adjusted op margin"; CFO Nicolelli: "You will see us... start to make additional investments" and "our AOPM forecast for the rest of the year will be similar... in that mid-19% range." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • David Koning, Baird: "Do you get some of them pushing you on price"; CEO Kapoor: "We are not really seeing clients come to us just asking for price reduction" and "the commercial model is also changing... towards a fixed fee... milestone-based payment and an outcome-based model." (CEO Kapoor)
  • David Koning, Baird: "Would you expect to see a little deceleration" internationally; CEO Kapoor: "We have very little and very limited exposure to the Middle East" and "we are not really seeing any direct impact due to the conflict as such." (CEO Kapoor)
  • Margaret Nolan, William Blair: "any perspective on net revenue retention"; CEO Kapoor: "on a net basis, we are seeing a much wider landscape to play in" and "the size of the operation actually increase despite providing them with a benefit." (CEO Kapoor)
  • Margaret Nolan, William Blair: "how [partnerships are] progressing"; CEO Kapoor: "the activity and the deal flow has increased substantially." (CEO Kapoor)
  • Surinder Thind, Jefferies: "help me understand the step down in the digital ops segment"; CFO Nicolelli: "As the adoption of AI increases, we are going to see there being a bigger shift towards data and AI-led operations" and "digital operations will likely continue to have the same kind of deceleration of growth." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • Surinder Thind, Jefferies: "turning to headcount"; CEO Kapoor: "our revenues have increased by 14%, and our headcount has increased by about 11%" and "you would expect the delta... to be 3% or so." (CEO Kapoor)
  • Abbey Hochreiner, JPMorgan: "specific drivers" for AI/data traction; CEO Kapoor: "we are seeing broad-based traction and growth across all of these different service lines" and "the challenge for us is actually being able to hire talent quick enough." (CEO Kapoor)
  • Abbey Hochreiner, JPMorgan: "10x harder" going from 50% to 80% automation; CEO Kapoor: "there's a whole series of work that needs to be undertaken" including "the data foundation" and "guardrails" and "token economics... very, very complex." (CEO Kapoor)
  • David Grossman, Stifel: "compare and contrast" NRR and durability; CEO Kapoor: "clients are actually getting more comfortable outsourcing more work and outsourcing more end-to-end process journeys." (CEO Kapoor)
  • David Grossman, Stifel: "can the NRR stay above 1"; CEO Kapoor: "I don't know. We'll have to see how that progresses." (CEO Kapoor)
  • Vincent Colicchio, Barrington: "what portion of new AI deals involve outcome-based pricing"; CEO Kapoor: "some of the AI deals have outcome-based pricing models" and "the biggest barrier... is clients don't have good metrics." (CEO Kapoor)
  • Vincent Colicchio, Barrington: "acquisition pipeline"; CEO Kapoor: "we are seeing a fairly strong pipeline of assets" and "we hope that we'd be able to kind of close an acquisition soon, but we can't really comment on the timing." (CEO Kapoor)

Sentiment analysis

  • "So good to see the raise" and repeated questions on "prudent outlook," pricing pressure, and digital ops deceleration kept analyst tone slightly positive but probing. (Bryan Bergin, TD Cowen; David Koning, Baird; Surinder Thind, Jefferies)
  • "We entered 2026 with strong momentum" alongside repeated caution language, including "we remain cautious about the current macroeconomic climate and geopolitical uncertainties." (CEO Kapoor; CFO Nicolelli)
  • Compared with Q4 2025, management tone stayed confident on AI demand ("we have excellent visibility" in Q1 2026 vs. "We have excellent visibility into 2026" in Q4 2025), while Q1 2026 Q&A leaned more toward mix shift mechanics ("digital operations... down 2%") and guidance prudence. (CEO Kapoor; CFO Nicolelli)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • "For full year 2026, we are increasing our revenue guidance to a range of $2.3 billion to $2.33 billion" versus the prior outlook of "$2.275 billion to $2.315 billion." (CEO Rohit Kapoor in Q1 2026; CEO Kapoor in Q4 2025)
  • "We are also increasing our adjusted diluted EPS to $2.18 to $2.23" versus the prior outlook of "$2.14 to $2.19." (CEO Kapoor in Q1 2026; CEO Kapoor in Q4 2025)
  • "Data and AI-led revenues... now represents 60% of the company total" versus "now represents 57% of total revenue." (CEO Kapoor in Q1 2026; CEO Kapoor in Q4 2025)
  • Analyst focus shifted from reconciling annual growth guidance and AI decision-making catalysts in Q4 2025 ("help us reconcile that") toward operational mix migration, pricing models, and headcount efficiency in Q1 2026 ("step down in the digital ops segment" and "outcome-based pricing"). (Bryan Bergin, TD Cowen; Surinder Thind, Jefferies; Vincent Colicchio, Barrington)

Risks and concerns

  • "We remain cautious about the current macroeconomic climate and geopolitical uncertainties." (CFO Nicolelli)
  • "Some of the industries where we see a little bit of softness is on retail and on communication." (Co-Founder, Chairman & CEO Rohit Kapoor)
  • "The challenge for us is actually being able to hire talent quick enough to be able to fulfill that demand." (CEO Kapoor)

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a start-of-year acceleration driven by scaled AI deployments, with the mix shift continuing by design as work migrates from digital operations into data and AI-led offerings. Executives emphasized that the commercial model is evolving toward fixed-fee, milestone-based, and outcome-based structures, while maintaining that client conversations are centered on measurable benefits rather than price cuts. With Q1 results, EXL raised full-year 2026 revenue and adjusted EPS guidance, while reiterating caution on macro and geopolitical uncertainty and signaling continued investment that keeps operating margin expectations around the mid-19% range.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: O-I Glass outlines 2026 adjusted EPS of $1.00-$1.50 as it targets at least $275M of Fit to Win benefits
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: O-I Glass (OI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Gordon Hardie said Q1 started “challenging,” with “demand… sluggish early in the quarter before improving through March,” and added that “first quarter adjusted earnings of $0.05 per share came in below our original expectations.” He highlighted pressure in Europe from “elevated commercial pressures… and several onetime external events that increased our costs,” while reiterating, “our strategy and priorities are unchanged.”
  • Hardie framed a sharper regional split: “In the Americas, earnings were stable,” while “in Europe, results fell short of expectations amid elevated competitive pressure,” adding that Europe is “earlier in the Fit to Win journey than the Americas.”
  • Hardie pointed to improving demand cadence and new wins: shipments were “down about 8% versus the prior year,” but “volume trends improved sequentially through the quarter, with March volumes down only 2%,” and the company has “15 confirmed incremental volume wins in hand… that should contribute 1.5% of new sales volume starting in the second half of the year.”
  • CFO John Haudrich reported: “First quarter net sales were $1.54 billion, essentially flat with the prior year,” and said results reflected “commercial headwinds, including unfavorable net price and lower volumes,” plus “an unusually high effective tax rate on low pretax earnings.”

Outlook

  • Haudrich said the company “updated our full year guidance to adjusted earnings of $1 to $1.50 per share,” citing Europe where the company “risk-adjusted our outlook by up to $25 million given elevated competitive pressures.”
  • Haudrich identified energy as the key variable: “macro-driven energy inflation… could total $75 million to $100 million,” while adding that “approximately 75% to 80% of gas requirements are protected” in Europe.
  • Hardie reiterated the volume setup: “we continue to expect full year sales volumes to be about flat with the prior year,” with shipments “stable in the second quarter” and “low to mid-single-digit growth in the second half.”

Financial Results

  • Haudrich said segment operating profit was “$142 million, down from $209 million last year,” driven by “commercial pressures.”
  • In the Americas, Haudrich said segment operating profit was “$142 million, essentially flat year-over-year,” and noted “$10 million of disruption-related expense driven by extreme weather, civil unrest in Mexico and a natural gas pipeline failure in Peru.”
  • In Europe, Haudrich said “profitability compressed meaningfully,” with segment operating profit “breakeven,” and attributed the decline primarily to “a $76 million reduction in net price,” also reflecting “the reset of favorable energy contracts that expired last year.”

Q&A

  • George Staphos, BofA Securities: asked why management and the Board were confident “the turn is happening in 2Q” and probed energy sensitivity and covenants; Senior VP & CFO Haudrich: said the guidance assumes “EUR 45 to EUR 55 per megawatt hour,” adding “for every EUR 5 drop on average, we get back about $0.05 per share,” and stated, “we’re not anywhere near at risk” on secured debt covenants, citing “$1.5 billion of liquidity.”
  • Michael Roxland, Truist: asked for a split of the “15 accounts” wins and end-market sources; CEO Hardie: said the 1.5% annualized volume is “split about 70%, 75% Americas, 25%, 30% Europe,” and added, “in North America, for the first time, we’re starting to make inroads into RTDs.”
  • Roxland, Truist: asked how the company still reaches the 2027 target after an implied 2026 setback; CEO Hardie: said the company remains “laser-focused on our 2027 Investor Day targets… $1.45 billion,” citing “$150 million of Fit to Win to come in 2027” and “price adjustment formulas that are lagged.”
  • Anthony Pettinari, Citi: asked about Southern Europe oversupply dynamics and share shifts; Senior VP & CFO Haudrich: said Europe capacity utilization is improving with “announced capacity closures underway,” and argued this can support “a much more supply-demand balance.”
  • Gaurav Sharma, UBS: asked for optimal European utilization and whether more sites could be idled; Senior VP & CFO Haudrich: said “something in the 90s, low 90s is a great place to be for a glass plant,” and described prior “13%, 14% excess capacity” reducing to “low single digits.”
  • Arun Viswanathan, RBC: pressed whether new wins can offset market declines and if consistent 1%-2% growth is achievable; CEO Hardie: said volumes “have been below what we thought,” but added, “we really feel we’ve bottomed out,” and described year-end volume expectations as “net.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative, with repeated pressure on the Europe “breakeven” outcome, the “steep climb” to 2027 targets, and questions on energy, utilization, and covenants.
  • Management: slightly positive in forward framing but defensive on Europe and energy, emphasizing control levers and hedging; Haudrich said, “we’re pretty confident” in the energy exposure range and Hardie said, “we are not satisfied with our first quarter results, and we are moving quickly to improve performance.”
  • Compared with Q4 2025, the tone shifted from “positive momentum” and an improving 2026 setup to a more cautious posture with an “updated… 2026 guidance” and heavier focus on Europe pricing and energy inflation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language softened: Q4 2025 projected 2026 adjusted EPS of “$1.65 to $1.90,” while Q1 2026 revised to “$1 to $1.50 per share.”
  • The earnings bridge shifted from Q4’s emphasis on Fit to Win momentum and an energy “step-up” to Q1’s emphasis on Europe net price pressure, “breakeven” profitability in Europe, and a larger near-term macro-energy swing factor.
  • Analysts pivoted from Q4 questions on volume cadence and cash flow levers to Q1 questions centered on Europe competitive intensity, energy sensitivities, and downside protection.

Risks and Concerns

  • Hardie said Europe faced “elevated competitive pressure” and Haudrich tied Europe’s Q1 compression to a “$76 million reduction in net price,” alongside low utilization, “most notably in wine in Southern Europe.”
  • Haudrich flagged uncertainty from “conflicts in the Middle East” driving “$75 million to $100 million” in energy inflation, while describing mitigation via hedging and contractual structures: “More than half of our business operates under contractual price adjustment formulas.”

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a below-expectations start driven by Europe pricing pressure and external cost events, while keeping the strategy centered on Fit to Win execution, network actions in Europe, and a new go-to-market push tied to 15 incremental account wins. The company revised 2026 adjusted EPS to $1-$1.50, framed energy inflation and European competitiveness as the main swing factors, and emphasized liquidity, hedging coverage, and lagged pricing mechanisms as key supports as it targets stronger second-half volume and momentum into 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: InvenTrust raises 2026 NAREIT FFO guidance to $2.00-$2.06 per share while targeting $300M net investment
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: InvenTrust Properties Corp. (IVT) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Daniel Busch): "Our first quarter results reflected steady operating performance across the portfolio. Same-property NOI grew 2.6%, while core FFO and NAREIT FFO per share increased 6.5% and 10.4%, respectively, from the first quarter of 2025."
  • Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch): "Given this visibility, coupled with increased confidence around our acquisition pipeline, we were able to increase FFO per share guidance for 2026."
  • Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch): "During the quarter, we completed $123 million towards our $300 million net investment guidance for the year, and we have another $167 million of additional deals awarded or under contract with other opportunities still in the pipeline."
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Michael Phillips): "Subsequent to quarter end in April, we signed a definitive note purchase agreement for a $250 million private placement of senior unsecured notes."
  • Quote (Executive VP, COO, General Counsel & Corporate Secretary Christy David): "We executed 64 leases covering approximately 329,000 square feet and comparable blended spreads were 10.5%, with new leases at 19.8% and renewals at 9.9%."

Outlook

  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "We are reaffirming our full year same-property NOI growth guidance range of 3.25% to 4.25%."
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "For NAREIT FFO, we are increasing our full year guidance range to $2 to $2.06 per share, which represents a 7.4% growth at the midpoint versus 2025."
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "Our core FFO guidance is increasing to $1.92 to $1.96 per share, up 6% at the midpoint from last year."
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "This increase is primarily driven by mark-to-market lease adjustments related to our recent acquisitions."

Financial results

  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "Same-property NOI for the quarter totaled $48.7 million, an increase of 2.6% over the first quarter of 2025."
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "NAREIT FFO for the quarter totaled $41.3 million or $0.53 per diluted share, reflecting a 10.4% increase from the first quarter of 2025. Core FFO rose 6.5% to $0.49 per share year-over-year."
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "We also recognized approximately $800,000 of lease termination fee income during the quarter, which was anticipated and incorporated into our initial guidance."
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "At quarter end, total liquidity stood at $346 million, including $27 million of cash and $319 million available on our revolving credit facility."
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "Net leverage finished the quarter at 29.7% and net debt to adjusted EBITDA was 5.2x on a trailing 12-month basis."

Q&A

  • Todd Thomas, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division: "Are those expected to close by roughly the end of the second quarter?" Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch): "I think we're hoping that most of those will close at some point in the second quarter... maybe leaking a little bit into the third quarter."
  • Todd Thomas, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc., Research Division: "How should we think about equity capital sort of fitting into the equation" Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch): "But having said that, it all is predicated on the opportunity set. And if the opportunity set is one where we can continue to grow cash flow accretively, we'll look at all different avenues."
  • Andrew Reale, BofA Securities, Research Division: "Do you have any additional Nashville assets in the pipeline currently?" Quote (Executive VP, COO, General Counsel & Corporate Secretary David): "We do have a few other assets in the pipeline. Nothing currently under LOI or near execution."
  • Cooper Clark, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: "I wanted to ask about the same-property NOI acceleration in the back half of the year" Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "We do expect 90% of that to be coming online by the end of the year... in Q3 and Q4 is when you'll see most of that come online."
  • Michael Gorman, BTIG, LLC, Research Division: "for those 7 larger format small shop tenants, was there anything thematic in there?" Quote (Executive VP, COO, General Counsel & Corporate Secretary David): "there's nothing systematic or thematic about what departed in that area."
  • Paulina Rojas Schmidt, Green Street Advisors, LLC, Research Division: "have you seen any shift in terms of cap rates?" Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch): "I think it's much of the same as opposed to any material difference from maybe last quarter or even a couple of quarters ago."

Sentiment analysis

  • Quote (Paulina Rojas Schmidt, Green Street Advisors, LLC, Research Division): "have you seen any shift in terms of cap rates?" and Quote (Cooper Clark, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division): "I wanted to ask about the same-property NOI acceleration" reflected a neutral-to-slightly skeptical tone focused on pricing, cadence, and conversion, while repeatedly pressing for timing and durability.
  • Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch): "we feel pretty good about our multiple. We feel good about where the stock is at" and Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch): "If we didn't have the demand right behind those, perhaps I would tell you that there would be some softness, but that's absolutely not the case" reflected slightly positive, confidence-forward framing in both prepared remarks and Q&A.
  • Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch) in Q4 2025: "The competition is still very strong" versus Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch) in Q1 2026: "Not really, to be frank" (on distress) suggested a similar competitive backdrop, with Q1 commentary leaning more toward execution visibility and pipeline depth.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips) in Q4 2025: "For NAREIT FFO, we are providing guidance in the range of $1.97 to $2.03 per share" versus Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips) in Q1 2026: "we are increasing our full year guidance range to $2 to $2.06 per share" highlighted a higher full-year range while keeping same-property NOI growth guidance unchanged.
  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips) in Q4 2025: "At year-end, total liquidity stood at $480 million" versus Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips) in Q1 2026: "total liquidity stood at $346 million" alongside Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "a $250 million private placement" emphasized a shift toward terming out capital as acquisition activity progressed.
  • Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch) in Q4 2025: "almost half of that $300 million is either -- has either been awarded or is under contract" versus Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch) in Q1 2026: "we completed $123 million" and "another $167 million" pointed to greater year-to-date conversion and a larger identified near-term closing set.

Risks and concerns

  • Quote (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Phillips): "These gains were partially offset by a 40 basis point headwind from bad debt and 60 basis points from an expected temporary impact in occupancy" framed near-term operational offsets to NOI growth.
  • Quote (Executive VP, COO, General Counsel & Corporate Secretary David): "Painted Tree Marketplace closed stores across the U.S., including our one location in Glen Allen, Virginia" and "representing approximately 30,000 square feet or about 20 basis points of ABR" identified a tenant-specific vacancy risk, with mitigation in Quote (Executive VP, COO, General Counsel & Corporate Secretary David): "We are well positioned to backfill this space."
  • Quote (CEO, President & Board Director Busch): "It continues -- like we're continuing to find really good opportunities, but there hasn't been a whole lot of distress on the seller side" underscored a competitive acquisition environment without distressed supply.

Final takeaway

Management described steady operating performance and raised 2026 FFO per-share guidance while reaffirming same-property NOI growth expectations, pointing to back-half NOI acceleration from signed-not-open commencements and continued embedded rent growth. Executives emphasized acquisition momentum toward the $300 million net investment plan, alongside a $250 million senior unsecured notes private placement intended to support capital deployment, while acknowledging temporary occupancy impact and specific tenant move-outs as near-term items they expect to re-lease at higher prospective rents.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Scotts Miracle-Gro targets 2030 $1B incremental sales and plans share buyback of at least 1/3 of shares
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Scotts Miracle-Gro Company (SMG) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "Through our first 6 months of the fiscal year, we continued on our growth trajectory and made progress toward every single one of our full year financial imperatives." (CEO & Chairman of the Board James Hagedorn) "They include closing the quarter with leverage at 3.71x debt-to-EBITDA... and the divestiture of Hawthorne." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

  • "We're ready to embark on the first tranche of the multiyear share repurchase program we announced last quarter and said would begin once leverage was comfortably in the 3s." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn) "The ultimate goal is to buy back at least 1/3 of our outstanding shares." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

  • "The 2030 target of an incremental $1 billion in top line sales, a gross margin rate approaching 40% and total EBITDA north of $1 billion." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn) "We think upwards of $800 million of top line sales growth under SMG 2.0 will be generated through e-commerce alone." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

  • "We're announcing the hiring of a Chief Brand Officer... The person we've selected has agreed to start in June." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn) "With Chris Hagedorn coming off Hawthorne, he'll be able to devote more time to our core business... Chris will lead company strategy with focus on business development." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

  • "So far in fiscal '26, we've introduced 83 new product SKUs, accounting for $41 million in revenue." (President & COO Nate Baxter) "We have line of sight to eliminate 30% of our lowest-performing SKUs by next fiscal year." (President & COO Baxter)

  • "In the second quarter, total company net sales increased 5% to $1.46 billion." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Mark Scheiwer) "E-commerce POS trends continue to demonstrate the effectiveness of our channel expansion... Year-to-date e-comm POS dollars were up 22%." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer)

Outlook

  • "As we look to the rest of the year, we're reaffirming our guidance and will not let commodities steer us off course despite global supply pressures from the Iran war." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn) "Fiscal '27 is a bigger unknown... we will... take pricing in fiscal '27 if necessary. We will not sacrifice our gross margin goals." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

  • "We're tracking to our targets for net sales growth, gross margin expansion and leverage reduction." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer) "We are reaffirming our fiscal '26 guidance and have a high degree of optimism for the long-term financial goals." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer)

  • "Urea specifically, we have flexibility... we're going to delay purchases a bit this year relative to how we've done it in the past." (President & COO Baxter) "We're committed to taking pricing if we have to." (President & COO Baxter)

Financial Results

  • "In the quarter, the GAAP and non-GAAP gross margin rate was 41.8%, a 280 basis point improvement and a 240 basis point improvement, respectively, over prior year." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer) "SG&A in the quarter increased 12%... The increase... was expected and reflects our increased media and marketing spend." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer)

  • "For the quarter, GAAP net income from continuing operations was $263.3 million or $4.46 per share." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer) "Adjusted non-GAAP net income... was $267.8 million or $4.53 per share." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer)

  • "We leverage at 3.71x... was a result of higher EBITDA and continued deployment of free cash flow to debt reduction." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer) "Year-to-date, free cash flow was favorable by more than $100 million over prior year." (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer)

Q&A

  • Jon Andersen, William Blair: "Could you talk a little bit about... the restage on the lawns business... and... e-com..."; President & COO Baxter: "Shipments remain strong... not concerned about inventory levels." John Sass: "We're... selling a 4-step type of solution... We're seeing another sell-through of our Halts... over 20%." Marketing Director Mike Davitt: "Consumers are moving to specifics... we're doing it in dot-com first."

  • Peter Grom, UBS: asked if SMG 2.0 build is "linear" and timing of margin actions; President & COO Baxter: "I would just look at it as linear. I don't think it will play out that way." CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn: "Just getting our share online up... will give Nate most of what he needs to get that $1 billion."

  • Peter Grom, UBS: asked why reiterate gross margin outlook; CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn: "We took the marketing money and said, if you want the marketing money, you're going to put it behind branded, and they did." Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer: "We definitely feel like we can deliver on the 32% gross margin guide." (Note: the $32% figure was stated in Q&A as "32% gross margin guide" with no additional numeric update.)

  • Jonathan Matuszewski, Jefferies: asked on raw material lock timing; Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer: "Generally... what you see in our P&L is stuff that was purchased most likely 6 to 9 months previously." President & COO Baxter: "We're going to delay purchases a bit this year."

  • Jonathan Matuszewski, Jefferies: asked on RONA shop-in-shops; President & COO Baxter: "I think it's a little early to really quantify the results from that... we'll have more data" by August.

  • Joseph Altobello, Raymond James: asked if pricing is "manageable" next year; CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn: "Yes, I absolutely feel." President & COO Baxter: "We've managed through it... I'm keeping my eye on the long ball."

  • Joseph Altobello, Raymond James: asked e-commerce margin/work cap impact; President & COO Baxter: "There is a margin delta... It's a few hundred basis points delta." Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer: "We're not going to be doing direct-to-consumer... we leverage our customers." CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn: "Not participating is suicide for us."

  • Christopher Carey, Wells Fargo: pressed on when '27 cost/pricing decisions must be made; CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn: "You're probably talking 8 to 12 weeks where these decisions need to be made." President & COO Baxter: "Q3... that's exactly when we have to make all these decisions."

  • William Reuter, BofA: asked if extra pricing was taken; President & COO Baxter: "We haven't taken any additional pricing since establishing with the retailers last quarter."

  • William Reuter, BofA: asked leverage posture with buybacks; CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn: "Yes." "Mark has knock it off rights here." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on commodities/pricing and margin durability, including "At what point is it that you have to kind of make decisions" and whether guidance was "simply conservatism." (Christopher Carey, Wells Fargo; Peter Grom, UBS)

  • Management tone slightly positive in prepared remarks and more forceful in Q&A on pricing and e-commerce: "We will not sacrifice our gross margin goals." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn) "Not participating is suicide for us." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

  • Versus last quarter, management’s Q2 language was more execution- and capital-return-focused ("We're there" on leverage; "move forward with the repurchases") after Hawthorne divestiture closed, while analyst questions shifted from early-season phasing to '27 commodity decision timing. (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn; Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q2, management said Hawthorne divestiture was completed ("completing its divestiture in early April"), compared with Q1 describing a pending sale and initial discontinued-operations impact. (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer; CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

  • Q2 introduced SMG 2.0 details and operating building blocks ("83 new product SKUs"; "eliminate 30% of our lowest-performing SKUs"; "about 40 use cases of AI"), while Q1 emphasized the new long-term targets and the initial repurchase authorization. (President & COO Baxter; CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn)

  • Q2 moved buybacks from authorization/timing to activation tied to leverage: Q1 said the $500 million program "will begin later in '26" and "begin in late 2026"; Q2 said "we're ready to embark on the first tranche" now that leverage is "comfortably in the 3s." (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn; Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Global supply pressures from the Iran war" and commodity volatility were framed as a fiscal '27 uncertainty, with mitigation via hedging, contingency plans, sourcing flexibility, and pricing: "Most of our cost of goods sold are locked" for fiscal '26, and "we will... take pricing" if needed for fiscal '27. (CEO & Chairman of the Board Hagedorn; Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer; President & COO Baxter)

  • E-commerce profit structure remains a stated challenge: "There is a margin delta... It's a few hundred basis points delta," with management pointing to product innovation and leveraging retail partners rather than building a direct-to-consumer network. (President & COO Baxter; Executive VP, CFO & Chief Accounting Officer Scheiwer)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q2 as the completion of key balance-sheet and portfolio milestones—"leverage at 3.71x" and the Hawthorne divestiture—while shifting toward capital returns and SMG 2.0 execution, including initiating the first tranche of a multiyear repurchase program targeting "at least 1/3" of shares, and pursuing a 2030 plan for "$1 billion" incremental sales with e-commerce positioned to generate "upwards of $800 million" of that growth, alongside an explicit stance that fiscal '27 commodity inflation will be managed with hedging, sourcing actions, and pricing to avoid sacrificing gross margin goals.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wall Street inches lower after Fed decision and now looks ahead to Big Tech earnings
data.description:
Wall Street trades lower on Wednesday as investors look ahead to big tech earnings and markets digest the latest Federal Reserve rate decision.

The blue chip Dow (DJI) was -0.7%, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was -0.3%, and the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.3%. 

From a sector-by-sector stance, nine of the 11 S&P segments were in the red, with materials at the bottom of the list. At the other end of the spectrum, energy was on top as oil (CL1:COM)  pushed back above $100 per barrel. 

U.S. Treasury yields topped 1-month highs. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 6 basis points to 4.41%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) ticked up 8 basis points to 3.93%. The 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) added 4 basis points to 4.98%. 

The Federal Open Market Committee kept its policy rate unchanged at 3.50%-3.75% for a third straight meeting, citing the uncertainty over the U.S. economic and global macro outlooks.

Elsewhere on the economic calendar, durable goods rebounded more than expected in March. Housing starts rose in March, while building permits fell. The U.S. international trade in goods deficit widened in March, and the wholesale and retail inventories accelerated M/M in March.

Big tech earnings data will continue to be in focus as Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT), and Amazon (AMZN) will release their results after the bell. 

“Stocks are again trading at record highs, reflecting strong investor confidence, but the S&P 500's heavy concentration in the Mag 7 technology leaders elevates downside risk should earnings fall short, as valuations leave little margin for error. For now, current levels are supported by solid forward growth expectations, but that justification could quickly erode if margin compression emerges, inflation weighs on growth, or earnings signal a broader slowdown,” Chris Brigati, chief investment officer of SWBC, stated.

data.title: Jerome Powell to stay on Fed board for a time after chair term ends
data.description:
Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said he'll continue to serve as governor for a time after his chair term ends in May, he said on Wednesday at his post-decision press conference.

"I will leave when it's appropriate to do so," he said.

Developing… Check back for updates.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Seacoast projects 2026 EPS of $2.48 to $2.52 while reiterating 53% to 55% efficiency ratio target
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Seacoast Banking Corporation of Florida (SBCF) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Charles Shaffer said the quarter was “highlighted by robust deposit growth, particularly in noninterest-bearing deposits, meaningful expansion in the net interest margin, and solid progress towards the financial guidance we introduced last quarter,” while noting Q1 loan growth was “seasonally softer and further impacted by elevated payoffs” but the pipeline “remains strong.”
  • Shaffer highlighted fee businesses and the Villages expansion, saying, “Noninterest income continued to perform well, driven by strength across wealth management, insurance, treasury and our mortgage businesses,” and added, “our expansion in the villages is already delivering results with solid mortgage production and growing demand for wealth management services.”
  • CFO Tracey Dexter framed reported vs. adjusted profitability and the securities action: “Seacoast reported net income of $31.9 million, or $0.29 per share in the first quarter,” and “Reported results include a $39.5 million pretax loss related to the strategic repositioning of a portion of our available-for-sale securities portfolio, which we executed in January.”
  • Dexter emphasized funding and margin drivers, saying, “Expansion in the net interest margin was a highlight this quarter, driven largely by lower deposit costs and the bond portfolio restructure,” and added that the company “were more active in share repurchases, buying back over 317,000 shares.”

Outlook

  • Management reiterated full-year adjusted EPS guidance, with CEO Charles Shaffer stating, “we continue to expect full year earnings per share in a range of $2.48 to $2.52 despite two less rate cuts.”
  • CFO Tracey Dexter said, “On Slide 17, we reiterate the guidance we provided last quarter,” adding, “The adjusted earnings per share outlook remains unchanged at $2.48 to $2.52, with the potential for slightly lower revenue resulting from the change in previously expected rate cuts, but with no change to bottom line results.”
  • In Q&A, management tied expense and integration cadence to the efficiency outlook. Chief Strategy Officer Michael Young said the company expects “an expense step-up here in the second quarter with our normal annual pay cycle,” and “we'll see the cost out come in the back half of the year,” while CEO Shaffer pointed back to guidance: “We think a full year efficiency ratio is somewhere between 53% and 55%.”

Financial Results

  • CFO Tracey Dexter said, “On an adjusted basis, net income was $67.8 million, or $0.62 per share, increasing 42% from the prior quarter, and 111% year-over-year,” and attributed the improvement to “expanding net interest income, disciplined balance sheet management and continued execution on organic growth initiatives.”
  • Dexter reported net interest income of $178.2 million and said “The net interest margin expanded 17 basis points to 3.83%,” while adding that “excluding the impact of accretion on acquired loans, margin expanded 13 basis points to 3.57%.”
  • Dexter described fee lines and the securities-loss impact on reported noninterest income: “Reported noninterest income was a net loss of $12.6 million,” while “Adjusted noninterest income, which excludes the securities repositioning, totaled $26.9 million.”
  • On balance sheet growth and credit, Dexter said “Total deposits increased $382 million during the quarter,” and that nonperforming loans increased “to 0.75% of total loans,” while stating the move to nonaccrual for two credits had “collateral values well in excess of balances outstanding and therefore, no credit loss is expected.”

Q&A

  • Wood Lay, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc.: asked how Q2 pipeline looks amid uncertainty and how much Q1 deposit strength is seasonal; Chairman, President & CEO Shaffer responded that Q1 had “three larger credits pay off in aggregate, $150 million,” but “the pipeline remains strong” and the company expects to “return to high single digits,” while Chief Strategy Officer Young said seasonal tax outflows are normal but management expects to “hold higher levels of noninterest-bearing deposits as we move forward.”
  • Russell Elliott Gunther, Stephens Inc.: asked about margin trajectory, deposit-cost direction without Fed cuts, and how efficiency flexes with revenue; Chief Strategy Officer Young said, “we do expect continued margin expansion here in the second and third quarter,” and noted, “we revised the revenue guidance low end down by 1 percentage point,” while CEO Shaffer said if revenue is higher “that does fall to the bottom line” and the company may “tighten a little bit on the expense side” while remaining “100% confident in delivering the EPS range.”
  • Liam Coohill, Raymond James & Associates: asked where growth is coming from and whether wealth balances can grow into Q2; CEO Shaffer said growth is “broad-based” with momentum in “The Villages and the expansionary markets,” and added on wealth, “we expect to continue to grow throughout the year,” citing “almost $17 million of new AUM coming out of The Villages.”
  • Kyle Gierman, Hovde Group: asked on banker hiring progress and M&A appetite; CEO Shaffer said on banker additions, “We're about halfway there,” and on M&A, “At the moment, it's heads down, focused on integration,” adding the company remains “focused only on Florida from an M&A perspective.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on payoffs, macro uncertainty, deposit-cost direction without Fed cuts, and the Villages cost-save cadence, including questions framed around “macro uncertainty,” “Fed on pause,” and expense “glide path.”
  • Management: slightly positive, leaning confident on guidance and integration execution, including CEO Shaffer’s “remain confident in the guidance,” “100% confident in delivering the EPS range,” and “we're going to deliver a flawless conversion.”
  • Versus last quarter: the tone stayed confident on the same EPS range, but the current call added more emphasis on rate-cut expectations and how that changes revenue assumptions, with Young explicitly stating, “we revised the revenue guidance low end down by 1 percentage point,” while maintaining the same bottom-line EPS guidance.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance messaging remained anchored to the same adjusted EPS range of $2.48 to $2.52, with Q1 emphasizing that it holds “despite two less rate cuts,” compared with Q4’s framing around exiting 2026 post-conversion with profitability targets.
  • Strategic emphasis moved from Q4’s acquisition close and securities-portfolio actions to Q1’s execution phase, with Q1 highlighting January’s AFS repositioning loss and reinvestment, and management repeatedly focusing on the summer Villages conversion timeline.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from Q4’s deeper questions on purchase accounting and additional securities restructures to Q1’s questions on payoffs, pipeline durability, deposit mix, expense cadence, and conversion-linked cost saves.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged elevated payoffs as a near-term headwind, with CEO Charles Shaffer saying, “payoffs were very elevated,” while mitigating with “the pipeline remains strong” and expectations that “payoffs [will] moderate in the coming quarters.”
  • The company tied part of its revenue sensitivity to fewer rate cuts, with Chief Strategy Officer Michael Young stating, “Without Fed cuts... we could see some stabilizing to increasing deposit costs potentially later this year.”
  • Integration and execution risk around the Villages conversion was a prominent theme, with CEO Shaffer stating, “We want to get this absolutely 100% right,” and “we're going to deliver a flawless conversion.”

Final Takeaway

Seacoast’s management positioned Q1 as progress toward the guidance introduced last quarter, emphasizing deposit-led funding improvement and margin expansion while holding the full-year adjusted EPS range of $2.48 to $2.52 even as the company adjusted revenue assumptions for fewer expected rate cuts. Executives underscored disciplined expenses alongside a conversion-driven cost-save cadence, with the Villages integration described as the near-term priority, while payoffs and the rate environment were identified as key variables that could influence loan growth and deposit-cost trends through 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
